stats.headline: Part 1 of two: **three grammar rule families** tested in almost every PSLE English Booklet A — tag questions, noun number rules, and pronouns.

keyConcepts.title: Welcome to Grammar MCQ — Part 1
keyConcepts.body: We'll cover **three grammar rule families** that show up in almost every PSLE English Booklet A. After each rule, you get an interactive question to try — pick an answer, hit Submit, and see if you're right.
bullets: **1. Tag questions** — "…, isn't he?" / "…, shall we?"
**2. Noun number rules** — subject-verb agreement + **few/little/many/much**
**3. Pronouns** — reflexive, possessive standalone, relative
keyConcepts.callout: Recognise the rule type from the options, then apply.

bullets: First are **1. Tag questions** — like "…, isn't he?" / or "…, shall we?"
Next up, **2. Noun number rules** — covering subject-verb agreement + words like **few/little/many/much**
Finally, **3. Pronouns** — including reflexive, possessive standalone, and relative ones.

keyConcepts.title: 1: Tag questions — the mirror rule
keyConcepts.body: A tag question is the little check at the end of a statement: **isn't he?**, **would she?**, **shall we?** The tag must **mirror** the verb/auxiliary of the main statement and **flip the polarity**.
bullets: **Step 1 — Find the main verb/auxiliary.** "She **has** been busy" → tag uses **has**. "He **gave up**" (past simple) → tag uses **did**.
**Step 2 — Flip the polarity.** Positive statement → **negative** tag ("She is busy, **isn't** she?"). Negative statement → **positive** tag ("He hasn't called, **has** he?").
**Step 3 — Match the pronoun.** "Tom" → **he**. "Mary" → **she**. "The team" → **it** (or **they** depending on context).

**Three special cases that catch most students:**
   • **"Let's"** → always **"shall we?"** (not "will we?")
   • **"I am"** → **"aren't I?"** (irregular)
   • **Negative-meaning words** (never, hardly, seldom, scarcely, nothing) make the statement NEGATIVE → use a **positive** tag
Finally, **Step 3 — Match the pronoun.** The name "Tom" becomes → **he**. The name "Mary" becomes → **she**. And "The team" becomes → **it** (or **they** depending on context).

keyConcepts.title: Try it — Tag questions

interactiveQuiz.label: PSLE 2023 Q1 — the "Let's" special case
interactiveQuiz.stem: "Let's play a game of hide-and-seek, ___?" my sister suggested.
interactiveQuiz.explanation: **"Let's"** is a fixed idiom — the tag is ALWAYS **"shall we?"**. The other options don't pair with "Let's" in English.

interactiveQuiz.label: PSLE 2024 Q8 — negative-stem polarity flip
interactiveQuiz.stem: "She **never ever** visits the museum, ___?" asked Mrs Lee.
interactiveQuiz.explanation: The verb **"visits"** is present simple → the tag uses **do/does** (eliminates *hasn't / won't / will*). The word **"never"** makes the statement NEGATIVE, so the tag flips to POSITIVE → **"does she"** (not "doesn't she").

keyConcepts.callout: **Mirror the verb, flip the polarity.** Watch for "never / hardly / seldom" — they make a statement negative even without the word "not".

keyConcepts.title: 2: Noun number — countable vs uncountable
keyConcepts.body: English splits nouns into **countable** (you can count them: **3 books, 5 ideas**) and **uncountable** (you can't: **water, advice, luck**). Each takes a different family of quantifiers.
bullets: **Countable + plural:** **few, many, several, each, a number of, fewer**
**Uncountable:** **little, much, a great deal of, a large amount of, less**
**Either:** **some, any, enough, more, most, all**

**The treacherous "mass" nouns** — these LOOK countable but are uncountable:
   • **time, effort, trouble, advice, luck, news, information**
   • **equipment, furniture, luggage, money, knowledge, work**
For these, always use **little / much / a great deal of**.
**"a great DEAL of" (uncountable)** vs **"a large NUMBER of" (countable)** — the noun **deal** attaches to uncountables, **number** to countables.
For **Countable + plural:** nouns, use words like **few, many, several, each, a number of, and fewer**.
For **Uncountable:** nouns, use **little, much, a great deal of, a large amount of, and less**.
For **Either:** type, you can use **some, any, enough, more, most, and all**.
Now, **"a great DEAL of" (uncountable)** vs the phrase **"a large NUMBER of" (countable)** — the noun **deal** attaches to uncountables, while **number** attaches to countables.

keyConcepts.title: 2: Noun number — subject-verb agreement traps
keyConcepts.body: The verb must match the subject's number. PSLE tests **four shapes** where the real subject isn't obvious.
bullets: **Plural-looking but singular**: **news, luggage, mathematics, athletics** → use **is / has**. Example: "The news **has** been circulating since yesterday."
**"The number of X" (singular)** vs **"A number of X" (plural)**. "**The** number of pupils **is** rising." vs "**A** number of pupils **are** absent."
**Interrupters with "as well as / except / together with":** The verb still matches the main subject. "Everyone, except Sanjay and Aidan, **has** handed in their homework."
**An -ing verb used as a "thing" (the subject) is always SINGULAR.** Even when it sounds plural. "**Reading** different types of books **helps** me improve."
**"Neither X nor Y"** matches the noun CLOSEST to the verb. "Neither Jack **nor I** **am** going."

Third, beware of **Interrupters with "as well as / except / or together with":** Here, The verb still matches the main subject. For example, "Everyone, except Sanjay and Aidan, **has** handed in their homework."
Fourth, **An -ing verb used as a "thing" (the subject) is always SINGULAR.** This is true Even when it sounds plural. For instance, "**Reading** different types of books **helps** me improve."
Finally, the rule for **"Neither X nor Y"** is that the verb matches the noun CLOSEST to the verb. So we say, "Neither Jack **nor I** **am** going."

keyConcepts.title: Try it — Noun number

interactiveQuiz.label: PSLE 2023 Q5 — uncountable quantifier
interactiveQuiz.stem: He must have ___ luck as he has won the top prize for the past two years.
interactiveQuiz.explanation: **"Luck"** is UNCOUNTABLE → reject **many** and **a large number of** (both go with countables). Between **enough** and **a great deal of**, the sentence implies "a lot" not "just sufficient" → **a great deal of luck**.

interactiveQuiz.label: PSLE 2024 Q9 — interrupter subject-verb agreement
interactiveQuiz.stem: Everyone, except Sanjay and Aidan, ___ in their homework.
interactiveQuiz.explanation: The real subject is **"Everyone"** — a singular indefinite pronoun. The "except Sanjay and Aidan" part is an INTERRUPTER — it doesn't change the subject. Singular subject → singular verb → **has handed**.

keyConcepts.callout: **Test the noun, ignore the interrupter.** For quantifiers, ask "can I count this?" first. For SVA, find the REAL subject and ignore phrases starting with **except / as well as / together with / along with**.

keyConcepts.title: 3: Pronouns — three families to keep separate
keyConcepts.body: Pronouns get tested in three forms; the trap is usually choosing the right form for the role in the sentence.
bullets:
**Personal vs reflexive.** Use the reflexive form (**himself, herself, themselves, myself, yourself**) ONLY when the subject and object of the action are the SAME person.
   • ✓ "Mary ate the cake by **herself**." → Mary is the doer AND she ate alone (same person).
   • ✓ "Tom hurt **himself**." → Tom is the doer AND Tom got hurt (same person).
   • ✗ When the object is a DIFFERENT person, use a regular pronoun (him, her, them): "John gave the book to **her**" — not "to herself".

**Possessive — "his" / "her" vs "hers".** Two forms:
   • **Adjective form** (in front of a noun): **my, your, his, her, our, their** → "**her** car"
   • **Standalone form** (replaces the noun): **mine, yours, his, hers, ours, theirs** → "That car is **hers**."
   ⚠️ No apostrophe ever. "It's" = "it is". The possessive is **its**.

**Relative pronouns:**
   • **who** — subject ("the boy **who** is running")
   • **whom** — object ("the boy to **whom** I gave the book")
   • **whose** — possessive ("the boy **whose** bag is missing")
   • **which** — things, not people

**Indefinite pronouns** like **everyone, anybody, nobody, someone** are always SINGULAR — they take **is / has** (not are / have).
   • ✓ "**Everyone is** here."
   • ✗ "Everyone **are** here." → wrong.

keyConcepts.title: Try it — Pronouns

interactiveQuiz.label: PSLE 2023 Q4 — relative possessive
interactiveQuiz.stem: Mrs Phua is someone ___ opinions I truly value.
interactiveQuiz.explanation: The blank comes right BEFORE "opinions" — the opinions BELONG to Mrs Phua → POSSESSIVE relative. **who** is subject (wrong), **whom** is object (wrong), **which** is for things (wrong). **whose** is the possessive relative.

interactiveQuiz.label: PSLE 2020 Q2 — reflexive must match the SUBJECT
interactiveQuiz.stem: Trixie was tickled when she caught **Mr Loh** looking at his reflection in the mirror, smiling at ___.
interactiveQuiz.explanation: Who is doing the smiling? **Mr Loh**. Who is being smiled at? Also Mr Loh (his reflection). Subject = object → REFLEXIVE. Mr Loh is male → **himself** (not "herself", which would match Trixie — but Trixie isn't the one smiling).

keyConcepts.callout: **For relative pronouns**, substitute mentally — if "of him / her / them" fits, the answer is **whose**. **For reflexives**, identify WHO is doing the action — the reflexive must match that person, not the bystander.

keyConcepts.title: Let's get some practice!
keyConcepts.body: Time to put the three Part 1 rules together. This quiz pulls real PSLE-style Grammar MCQ questions across all 3 sub-topics — with PSLE source questions prioritised first.
bullets: **15 questions** — **5 questions per sub-topic**.
PSLE questions come first, then top-up from school exam papers.
After you submit, the AI flags which rule families you got wrong and suggests a focused follow-up quiz.
**Mistakes are saved** — we'll return to them in an auto-revision session in **1 week**.

cta.label: Click here to start your practice quiz

bullets: You will face **15 questions** — which is **5 questions per sub-topic**.
The PSLE questions come first, then we top-up from school exam papers.
Note that After you submit, the AI flags which rule families you got wrong and suggests a focused follow-up quiz.
Your **Mistakes are saved** — and we'll return to them in an auto-revision session in **1 week**.

keyWords.definition: A short check at the end of a statement (isn't he? / shall we?) that mirrors the main verb and flips the polarity.
A noun you can count: book/books, idea/ideas. Takes few/many/several.
A noun that has no plural: water, advice, luck. Takes little/much/a great deal of.
A pronoun that loops back to the subject: himself, herself, themselves. Use when subject = object.
who / whom / whose / which — links a noun to a description (the boy who runs).

subTopics.label: Tag questions
subTopics.description: Tag question structure ('isn't he?', 'shall we?') — mirroring the main verb and flipping the polarity. Includes negative-word traps (never, hardly, seldom) and irregular cases ('Let's…' → shall we, 'I am' → aren't I).

subTopics.label: Noun number rules
subTopics.description: Subject-verb agreement plus countable / uncountable quantifiers. Handles mass nouns (news, luggage, advice), 'the number of' vs 'a number of', interrupters (everyone except…), gerund subjects.

subTopics.label: Pronouns
subTopics.description: Reflexive (himself, herself), possessive standalone (his, hers), and relative pronouns (who/whom/whose/which). Tests when each form is required by the role of the pronoun in the sentence.
